modules.title: Python Fundamentals

sections.heading: Variables & Memory Management
sections.content: In Python, variables are references to objects in memory, not containers. When you write x = 10, Python creates an integer object 10 in memory and x points to it. This is why Python is called a 'dynamically typed' language — the variable itself has no type, the object it references does.
sections.code:
x = 10
y = x        # y points to the SAME object as x
x = 20       # x now points to a NEW object, y still points to 10
print(y)     # Output: 10
sections.tip: This is the #1 most asked Python concept in interviews. If interviewer asks 'Is Python pass by value or pass by reference?' — the answer is pass by object reference.

sections.heading: Mutable vs Immutable
sections.content: Immutable types (int, float, str, tuple, frozenset) cannot be changed after creation. Mutable types (list, dict, set) can be modified in place. This distinction matters because mutable objects can cause unexpected side effects when shared.
sections.code:
# Immutable — creates new object
a = 'hello'
b = a
a = a + ' world'
print(b)  # Output: hello — b is NOT affected

# Mutable — modifies SAME object
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
a.append(4)
print(b)  # Output: [1, 2, 3, 4] — b IS affected!
sections.tip: Always be careful when passing mutable objects to functions — changes inside the function affect the original.

sections.heading: Control Flow — Beyond Basics
sections.content: Modern Python offers concise control flow patterns that interviewers expect you to know.
sections.code:
# Ternary operator
status = 'adult' if age >= 18 else 'minor'

# Walrus operator (:=) — Python 3.8+
while (data := input()) != 'quit':
    print(data)

# Match-Case — Python 3.10+
def http_status(code):
    match code:
        case 200: return 'OK'
        case 404: return 'Not Found'
        case 500: return 'Server Error'
        case _: return 'Unknown'
sections.tip: Walrus operator and match-case show you know modern Python. Mention these in interviews to stand out.

sections.heading: List Comprehensions
sections.content: Interviewers judge your Python level by whether you use comprehensions or write verbose loops. But don't overuse them — if logic has more than 2 conditions, a regular loop is better.
sections.code:
# Filter even numbers
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]

# Flatten 2D list
matrix = [[1,2,3], [4,5,6], [7,8,9]]
flat = [num for row in matrix for num in row]

# Dictionary comprehension
word = 'interview'
char_count = {ch: word.count(ch) for ch in set(word)}

# Set comprehension
unique_lengths = {len(w) for w in ['hi', 'hello', 'hey']}
sections.tip: When NOT to use comprehensions: complex logic, try/except needed inside, or when readability suffers.

sections.heading: Loops — enumerate, zip, and unpacking
sections.content: Professional Python developers never use range(len(list)) to iterate. They use enumerate for index+value and zip for parallel iteration.
sections.code:
# enumerate — get index AND value
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f'{i}: {fruit}')

# zip — iterate multiple lists together
names = ['Alice', 'Bob']
scores = [85, 92]
for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f'{name}: {score}')

# Swap without temp variable
a, b = b, a
sections.tip: Using range(len()) instead of enumerate() is a red flag for interviewers — it shows you don't write Pythonic code.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes to Avoid
sections.content: Two mistakes that catch even experienced developers.
sections.code:
# Mistake 1: Mutable default arguments
# WRONG
def add_item(item, items=[]):
    items.append(item)
    return items
print(add_item('a'))  # ['a']
print(add_item('b'))  # ['a', 'b'] — BUG!

# CORRECT
def add_item(item, items=None):
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append(item)
    return items

# Mistake 2: Modifying list while iterating
# WRONG — skips elements
for n in nums:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        nums.remove(n)
# CORRECT
nums = [n for n in nums if n % 2 != 0]
sections.tip: Mutable default argument bug is the #1 Python gotcha question in interviews.

key_points: Variables are references to objects, not containers
Mutable objects (list, dict, set) can cause unexpected side effects when shared
Python is pass-by-object-reference (not value, not reference)
Use list comprehensions for simple transformations — loops for complex logic
Never use mutable default arguments in functions

modules.title: Data Types & Structures

sections.heading: Strings — More Than You Think
sections.content: Strings are immutable sequences. Every string operation creates a NEW string. Always use f-strings (Python 3.6+) — it's the modern standard.
sections.code:
text = '  Hello, World!  '
text.strip()       # 'Hello, World!'
text.split(',')    # ['  Hello', ' World!  ']
text.lower()       # '  hello, world!  '

# f-strings — always use these
name = 'Alice'
age = 25
print(f'{name} is {age} years old')
print(f'Price: {99.5:.2f}')  # Price: 99.50

# Reverse a string — interview favorite
reversed_str = 'hello'[::-1]  # 'olleh'
sections.tip: Three ways to reverse a string: slicing [::-1], reversed(), or manual loop. Know all three for interviews.

sections.heading: Lists vs Tuples
sections.content: Lists are mutable and slower. Tuples are immutable, faster, and use less memory. Use tuples when data shouldn't change.
sections.code:
# Tuple unpacking
coordinates = (10, 20)
x, y = coordinates

# Named tuples — better than regular tuples
from collections import namedtuple
Employee = namedtuple('Employee', ['name', 'age', 'dept'])
emp = Employee('Alice', 25, 'Engineering')
print(emp.name)  # 'Alice' — readable

# Tuples as dict keys (lists cannot be)
location_data = {(28.6, 77.2): 'Delhi', (19.0, 72.8): 'Mumbai'}
sections.tip: Tuples can be dictionary keys because they're immutable (hashable). Lists cannot.

sections.heading: Dictionaries — Most Important Structure
sections.content: Dictionaries use hash tables internally. Lookup is O(1) average. Always use .get() for safe access.
sections.code:
person = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
email = person.get('email', 'not provided')  # Safe — no KeyError

# Merging dicts (Python 3.9+)
merged = dict1 | dict2

# defaultdict — avoid KeyError
from collections import defaultdict
word_count = defaultdict(int)
for word in 'hello world hello'.split():
    word_count[word] += 1

# Counter — easiest counting
from collections import Counter
counts = Counter('hello world hello'.split())
print(counts.most_common(2))  # [('hello', 2), ('world', 1)]
sections.tip: dict.get() prevents KeyError. Counter preserves insertion order since Python 3.7+ for tie-breaking in most_common().

sections.heading: Sets — Unique Elements & Fast Lookup
sections.content: Sets provide O(1) membership testing. Use them when checking 'is this element in the collection?' repeatedly.
sections.tip: Creating empty set: use set() not {}. {} creates empty dict.

sections.heading: When to Use What
sections.content: Choosing the right data structure shows interviewer you think about performance.
sections.code:
# Decision Guide:
# Ordered, changeable     → list
# Ordered, unchangeable   → tuple (faster + safer)
# Key-value pairs         → dict (O(1) lookup)
# Unique values           → set (auto-dedup)
# FIFO queue              → collections.deque (O(1) both ends)
# Counting things         → collections.Counter
# Sorted data always      → sortedcontainers.SortedList (pip install)
sections.tip: sortedcontainers is a third-party library (pip install sortedcontainers). Everything else (deque, Counter) is built-in Python standard library.

key_points: Strings are immutable — every operation creates a new string
Use f-strings (not .format() or %) — modern standard
Tuples are faster and safer than lists when data shouldn't change
dict.get() prevents KeyError — always use for uncertain keys
Sets give O(1) lookup — convert list to set when checking membership

modules.title: Functions & OOP

sections.heading: Functions as First-Class Citizens
sections.content: In Python, functions are objects. You can pass them as arguments, return them, and assign them to variables.
sections.code:
# Functions as arguments
def apply(func, value):
    return func(value)
result = apply(str.upper, 'hello')  # 'HELLO'

# Lambda — anonymous functions
square = lambda x: x ** 2
sorted_people = sorted(people, key=lambda p: p['age'])

# Map, Filter
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, numbers))
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x%2 == 0, numbers))
sections.tip: Use lambda for short one-line functions only. For anything complex, define a proper function.

sections.heading: *args and **kwargs
sections.content: *args collects extra positional arguments into a tuple. **kwargs collects keyword arguments into a dict. Order in function: required, *args, keyword-only, **kwargs.
sections.code:
def flexible(required, *args, **kwargs):
    print(f'Required: {required}')
    print(f'Extra args: {args}')     # tuple
    print(f'Extra kwargs: {kwargs}')  # dict

flexible('hello', 1, 2, 3, name='Alice', age=25)
sections.tip: Interview trick: 'What's the difference between *args and **kwargs?' — args is tuple (positional), kwargs is dict (keyword).

sections.heading: Decorators
sections.content: Decorators modify function behavior without changing function code. Real-world uses: logging, caching, authentication, timing.
sections.code:
def timer(func):
    import time
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f'{func.__name__} took {time.time()-start:.4f}s')
        return result
    return wrapper

@timer
def slow_function():
    import time
    time.sleep(1)
    return 'done'

slow_function()  # slow_function took 1.0012s
sections.tip: Python's built-in @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod are all decorators. Know how to write custom ones.

sections.heading: OOP — Classes & Objects
sections.content: Python classes support class variables (shared), instance variables (unique), properties (pythonic getters/setters), and three types of methods.
sections.tip: @property replaces getters/setters. @classmethod accesses class (cls). @staticmethod is just a utility — no access to class or instance.

sections.heading: Inheritance & Magic Methods
sections.content: Magic methods (dunder methods) let you define how objects behave with operators and built-in functions.
sections.tip: __len__ should ONLY be used for containers to represent 'number of items'. For numeric types use __int__ or __float__.

key_points: Functions are first-class objects — pass them around freely
*args = tuple of extra positional args, **kwargs = dict of extra keyword args
Decorators wrap functions to add behavior
Use @property instead of getters/setters — it's Pythonic
__repr__ is for developers, __str__ is for users

modules.title: File Handling & Error Management

sections.heading: File Operations
sections.content: Always use 'with' statement for files — it auto-closes even if errors occur. Never use manual open/close.
sections.code:
# Reading
with open('data.txt', 'r') as file:
    for line in file:         # Line by line — memory efficient
        print(line.strip())

# Writing
with open('output.txt', 'w') as f:  # 'w' overwrites, 'a' appends
    f.write('Hello World\n')

# CSV
import csv
with open('data.csv', 'r') as f:
    for row in csv.DictReader(f):
        print(row['name'])

# JSON
import json
with open('config.json', 'r') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
with open('output.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
sections.tip: csv and json are built-in Python modules — no pip install needed.

sections.heading: Exception Handling
sections.content: Catch specific exceptions, not bare except. Use else for success code and finally for cleanup.
sections.tip: Never use bare 'except:' — it catches even keyboard interrupts and system exits.

sections.heading: Context Managers & Logging
sections.content: Context managers handle setup and cleanup automatically. Logging is better than print() for production code.
sections.code:
# Custom context manager
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def db_connection(name):
    print(f'Connecting to {name}')
    conn = {'connected': True}
    try:
        yield conn
    finally:
        print(f'Closing {name}')
        conn['connected'] = False

with db_connection('mydb') as conn:
    print('Using database')

# Logging
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info('Processing started')
logger.error('Something failed', exc_info=True)
sections.tip: Use logging instead of print() in any serious project. It's built-in and configurable.

key_points: Always use 'with' statement for files — never manual open/close
Catch specific exceptions, not bare except:
Use custom exceptions for domain-specific errors
else in try block runs only when no exception occurs
Logging > print() — always use logging in production

modules.title: Libraries & Real Projects

sections.heading: Important Note
sections.content: numpy, pandas, and requests are external libraries — install with: pip install numpy pandas requests. In contrast, os, re, csv, json, logging are built-in Python — no installation needed.
sections.code:
# Install external libraries
# pip install numpy pandas requests

# These are built-in — no install needed:
import os, re, csv, json, logging
sections.tip: In interviews, mention whether a library is built-in or third-party. It shows you understand the Python ecosystem.

sections.heading: NumPy — Fast Numerical Computing
sections.content: NumPy operations are 10-100x faster than Python loops because they run in C internally.
sections.code:
import numpy as np

arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
doubled = arr * 2           # [2, 4, 6, 8, 10] — no loop!
print(arr.mean())           # 3.0
print(arr.std())            # Standard deviation

# Reshaping
matrix = np.arange(12).reshape(3, 4)

# Boolean indexing
scores = np.array([85, 92, 78, 95, 60])
passed = scores[scores >= 80]  # [85, 92, 95]
sections.tip: NumPy is the foundation of all data science in Python. Pandas, scikit-learn, TensorFlow all use NumPy arrays internally.

sections.heading: Pandas — Data Analysis
sections.content: Pandas is the most used library for data manipulation. groupby, merge, and pivot_table are interview favorites.
sections.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv')
df.head()              # First 5 rows
df.info()              # Column types, nulls
df.describe()          # Statistical summary

# Filter
high_salary = df[df['salary'] > 700000]

# Groupby
avg_by_city = df.groupby('city')['salary'].mean()

# Handle missing data
df.fillna(0)           # Fill NaN with 0
df.dropna()            # Remove rows with NaN
sections.tip: Pandas interview questions focus on groupby, merge, handling missing data, and apply/lambda combinations.

sections.heading: Requests — API Calls
sections.content: The requests library makes HTTP calls simple. Always handle timeouts and errors.
sections.tip: Always set timeout in requests.get() — without it, your program can hang forever.

sections.heading: Automation Scripts
sections.content: Python excels at automation — file renaming, data processing, email validation.
sections.tip: Automation scripts are great portfolio projects. Show 2-3 in interviews to prove practical Python skills.

key_points: NumPy for numerical work — 10-100x faster than Python lists
Pandas for data analysis — groupby and merge are interview favorites
requests for API calls — always handle timeouts
os and pathlib for file system operations
re (regex) for pattern matching and validation

modules.title: Interview Questions & Answers

sections.heading: Q1: is vs == difference
sections.content: == checks value equality. 'is' checks identity (same object in memory). Use 'is' only for None checks.
sections.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
print(a == b)   # True — same values
print(a is b)   # False — different objects

c = a
print(a is c)   # True — same object

# Correct None check
if x is None:   # Good
if x == None:   # Bad
sections.tip: Small integers (-5 to 256) are cached: a=5; b=5; a is b is True. Don't rely on this — it's CPython implementation detail.

sections.heading: Q2: GIL (Global Interpreter Lock)
sections.content: GIL allows only ONE thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. Multi-threaded programs don't achieve true parallelism for CPU-bound tasks.
sections.code:
# CPU-bound → use multiprocessing
from multiprocessing import Pool
with Pool(4) as p:
    results = p.map(heavy_function, data)

# I/O-bound → threading works fine
import threading
t = threading.Thread(target=download, args=(url,))
t.start()

# Modern async I/O
import asyncio
async def fetch(url):
    pass  # non-blocking I/O
sections.tip: GIL is released during I/O operations — that's why threading works for network/file operations.

sections.heading: Q3: Generators and yield
sections.content: Generators produce values one at a time using yield, saving memory. They're lazy — they compute values only when asked.
sections.tip: Use generators when processing large datasets that don't fit in memory.

sections.heading: Q4: Shallow vs Deep Copy
sections.content: Shallow copy copies outer structure but shares nested objects. Deep copy copies everything recursively.
sections.code:
import copy

original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]

# Shallow — inner lists shared
shallow = copy.copy(original)
shallow[0][0] = 99
print(original[0][0])  # 99 — affected!

# Deep — fully independent
original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
deep = copy.deepcopy(original)
deep[0][0] = 99
print(original[0][0])  # 1 — NOT affected

# Three ways to shallow copy a list:
# 1. old[:]  2. old.copy()  3. list(old)
sections.tip: list(old) is also a valid shallow copy method — frequently seen in code reviews.

sections.heading: Q5: Memory Management
sections.content: Python uses reference counting + cyclic garbage collector. When reference count reaches 0, memory is freed. GC handles circular references.
sections.code:
import sys

a = [1, 2, 3]
print(sys.getrefcount(a))  # Shows reference count

# Small integer caching
a = 5
b = 5
print(a is b)  # True — cached (-5 to 256)

a = 500
b = 500
print(a is b)  # False — not cached
sections.tip: Python caches small integers (-5 to 256) and short strings. This is CPython optimization, not guaranteed in other implementations.

key_points: 'is' checks identity, '==' checks equality — use 'is' only for None
GIL prevents true multithreading — use multiprocessing for CPU tasks
Generators save memory — use for large datasets
Shallow copy shares nested objects, deep copy is fully independent
__slots__ saves memory when creating many instances

modules.title: Big-O, Collections & itertools

sections.heading: Big-O Time Complexity — The Cheat Sheet
sections.content: Understanding Big-O is what separates a coder from an engineer. Every data structure choice has performance implications. Interviewers at Google, Amazon, and Microsoft expect you to justify your choice of data structure using Big-O.
sections.code:
# LIST operations:
# append()      → O(1)
# insert(0, x)  → O(N) — shifts all elements!
# pop()         → O(1) — from end
# pop(0)        → O(N) — shifts all elements!
# x in list     → O(N) — scans entire list
# list[i]       → O(1) — direct index access
# sort()        → O(N log N)

# DICT operations:
# dict[key]     → O(1) average
# key in dict   → O(1) average
# dict.get()    → O(1) average
# del dict[key] → O(1) average

# SET operations:
# x in set      → O(1) average
# set.add()     → O(1) average
# set.remove()  → O(1) average

# DEQUE operations (from collections):
# appendleft()  → O(1) — list insert(0) is O(N)!
# popleft()     → O(1) — list pop(0) is O(N)!
sections.tip: When interviewer asks 'check if element exists' — NEVER use list. Convert to set first. list is O(N), set is O(1). This single optimization can make your code 1000x faster.

sections.heading: When Big-O Matters — Real Example
sections.content: Choosing wrong data structure can make code 1000x slower. Here's a real interview scenario.
sections.tip: In interviews, always say: 'I'll convert this to a set for O(1) lookup' before writing the code. It shows you think about performance.

sections.heading: itertools — Solve Complex Problems in 2 Lines
sections.content: itertools provides C-optimized tools for iteration. Using these in interviews instantly signals expert-level Python knowledge.
sections.code:
from itertools import groupby, chain, permutations, combinations, product, islice

# chain — flatten multiple iterables
combined = list(chain([1,2], [3,4], [5,6]))  # [1,2,3,4,5,6]

# groupby — group consecutive elements
data = [('A', 1), ('A', 2), ('B', 3), ('B', 4), ('A', 5)]
sorted_data = sorted(data, key=lambda x: x[0])  # MUST sort first!
for key, group in groupby(sorted_data, key=lambda x: x[0]):
    print(key, list(group))
# A [('A', 1), ('A', 2), ('A', 5)]
# B [('B', 3), ('B', 4)]

# permutations — all possible arrangements
list(permutations('ABC', 2))  # [('A','B'), ('A','C'), ('B','A')...]

# combinations — unique selections (order doesn't matter)
list(combinations('ABC', 2))  # [('A','B'), ('A','C'), ('B','C')]

# product — cartesian product (nested loop replacement)
list(product('AB', '12'))  # [('A','1'), ('A','2'), ('B','1'), ('B','2')]

# islice — slice a generator without converting to list
from itertools import islice
first_5 = list(islice(range(1000000), 5))  # [0,1,2,3,4] — no memory waste
sections.tip: groupby REQUIRES sorted input. This is the #1 mistake developers make with groupby — it only groups CONSECUTIVE equal elements.

sections.heading: collections — Beyond dict and list
sections.content: The collections module has specialized containers that solve common patterns better than basic data types.
sections.code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict, Counter, OrderedDict, ChainMap

# deque — O(1) operations on both ends
queue = deque([1, 2, 3])
queue.appendleft(0)    # O(1) — list.insert(0,x) is O(N)!
queue.popleft()        # O(1) — list.pop(0) is O(N)!
queue.rotate(1)        # [3, 0, 1, 2] — rotate elements

# defaultdict — auto-initialize missing keys
grouped = defaultdict(list)
for name, dept in [('Alice','Eng'), ('Bob','Sales'), ('Charlie','Eng')]:
    grouped[dept].append(name)
# {'Eng': ['Alice', 'Charlie'], 'Sales': ['Bob']}

# Counter — count anything
words = 'the cat sat on the mat the cat'.split()
c = Counter(words)
print(c.most_common(2))  # [('the', 3), ('cat', 2)]
print(c['the'])          # 3

# Counter arithmetic
c1 = Counter(a=3, b=1)
c2 = Counter(a=1, b=2)
print(c1 + c2)  # Counter({'a': 4, 'b': 3})
print(c1 - c2)  # Counter({'a': 2}) — drops zero/negative

# ChainMap — search multiple dicts as one
defaults = {'color': 'red', 'size': 'M'}
user_prefs = {'color': 'blue'}
config = ChainMap(user_prefs, defaults)
print(config['color'])  # 'blue' — user_prefs checked first
print(config['size'])   # 'M' — falls back to defaults
sections.tip: deque is pronounced 'deck'. Using list as a queue is a common anti-pattern — deque is the correct choice for FIFO operations.

key_points: list lookup is O(N), set/dict lookup is O(1) — always convert for membership checks
deque for O(1) operations on both ends — never use list as a queue
itertools.groupby requires sorted input — most common mistake
Counter supports arithmetic — add, subtract, intersect counters
defaultdict eliminates KeyError checks — cleaner code

modules.title: Concurrency & Async Programming

sections.heading: The Concurrency Trifecta — When to Use What
sections.content: This is the #1 architectural question in senior Python interviews: 'I have a slow script. How do I make it faster?' The answer depends on whether your task is CPU-bound or I/O-bound.
sections.code:
# CPU-BOUND (heavy computation)
# → Use MULTIPROCESSING (separate processes, bypass GIL)
# Examples: image processing, ML training, data crunching

# I/O-BOUND (waiting for external resources)
# → Use THREADING or ASYNCIO
# Examples: API calls, file downloads, database queries

# Decision Tree:
# Is it CPU-heavy?  → multiprocessing
# Is it I/O-heavy with few connections?  → threading
# Is it I/O-heavy with 1000+ connections? → asyncio
sections.tip: The interviewer wants to hear you say: 'It depends on whether the task is CPU-bound or I/O-bound.' This single sentence shows architectural thinking.

sections.heading: Threading — For I/O-Bound Tasks
sections.content: Threading runs multiple tasks concurrently (not parallel) within a single process. GIL is released during I/O, so threads work great for network calls, file operations, and database queries.
sections.code:
import threading
import time

# Without threading — sequential (slow)
def download(url):
    print(f'Downloading {url}')
    time.sleep(2)  # Simulate network delay
    print(f'Done {url}')

# Sequential: 3 downloads × 2 seconds = 6 seconds
for url in ['url1', 'url2', 'url3']:
    download(url)

# With threading — concurrent (fast)
threads = []
for url in ['url1', 'url2', 'url3']:
    t = threading.Thread(target=download, args=(url,))
    threads.append(t)
    t.start()

for t in threads:
    t.join()  # Wait for all to finish
# Total time: ~2 seconds instead of 6!

# Using ThreadPoolExecutor (cleaner)
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=5) as executor:
    urls = ['url1', 'url2', 'url3', 'url4', 'url5']
    results = executor.map(download, urls)
sections.tip: ThreadPoolExecutor is the modern way to use threads. Never manage raw threads in production — use the executor pattern.

sections.heading: Multiprocessing — For CPU-Bound Tasks
sections.content: Multiprocessing creates separate Python processes, each with its own GIL. This is the ONLY way to achieve true parallelism in Python for CPU-intensive tasks.
sections.code:
from multiprocessing import Pool, cpu_count
import time

def heavy_computation(n):
    """Simulate CPU-heavy work"""
    total = sum(i * i for i in range(n))
    return total

# Sequential — uses 1 CPU core
start = time.time()
results = [heavy_computation(10_000_000) for _ in range(4)]
print(f'Sequential: {time.time()-start:.2f}s')

# Parallel — uses ALL CPU cores
start = time.time()
with Pool(cpu_count()) as pool:
    results = pool.map(heavy_computation, [10_000_000] * 4)
print(f'Parallel: {time.time()-start:.2f}s')

# On 4-core machine:
# Sequential: ~8 seconds
# Parallel: ~2 seconds (4x faster!)

print(f'Your CPU has {cpu_count()} cores')
sections.tip: Multiprocessing has overhead for creating processes. Don't use it for small tasks — the process creation time may exceed the computation time.

sections.heading: Asyncio — For High-Concurrency I/O
sections.content: Asyncio handles thousands of concurrent I/O operations in a single thread. It's event-driven — while one task waits for I/O, others run. Perfect for web servers, API scrapers, and chat applications.
sections.code:
import asyncio

# Basic async function
async def fetch_data(url, delay):
    print(f'Fetching {url}...')
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)  # Non-blocking wait
    print(f'Done {url}')
    return f'Data from {url}'

# Run multiple async tasks concurrently
async def main():
    # gather runs all tasks concurrently
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_data('api/users', 2),
        fetch_data('api/orders', 3),
        fetch_data('api/products', 1)
    )
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())
# Total time: ~3 seconds (longest task)
# Sequential would be: 6 seconds

# Real-world: async HTTP with aiohttp
# pip install aiohttp
import aiohttp

async def fetch_url(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        return await response.json()

async def scrape_all():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        urls = [f'https://api.example.com/page/{i}' for i in range(100)]
        tasks = [fetch_url(session, url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return results
sections.tip: Use asyncio when you have 100+ concurrent I/O operations. For 5-10 operations, threading is simpler and equally effective.

sections.heading: Interview Red Flags — Python Anti-Patterns
sections.content: Senior interviewers watch for these common mistakes that show lack of experience.
sections.code:
# RED FLAG 1: Using list as queue
# WRONG
queue = []
queue.append('task1')    # OK
queue.pop(0)             # O(N) — shifts all elements!
# CORRECT: Use deque
from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append('task1')
queue.popleft()          # O(1)

# RED FLAG 2: String concatenation in loop
# WRONG — creates new string each iteration O(N²)
result = ''
for word in words:
    result += word + ' '
# CORRECT — join is O(N)
result = ' '.join(words)

# RED FLAG 3: Not using context managers
# WRONG
f = open('file.txt')
data = f.read()
f.close()  # What if error occurs before close?
# CORRECT
with open('file.txt') as f:
    data = f.read()  # Auto-closes even on error

# RED FLAG 4: Checking type with ==
# WRONG
if type(x) == int:
# CORRECT — handles inheritance
if isinstance(x, int):

# RED FLAG 5: Using global variables
# WRONG
counter = 0
def increment():
    global counter  # Avoid this!
    counter += 1
# CORRECT — use class or pass as argument
sections.tip: If an interviewer sees you use list.pop(0) instead of deque.popleft(), or string += in a loop instead of join(), they'll immediately mark you as junior level.

key_points: CPU-bound → multiprocessing, I/O-bound → threading or asyncio
ThreadPoolExecutor is the modern way to use threads in production
Multiprocessing bypasses GIL by creating separate processes
asyncio is best for 100+ concurrent I/O operations
String concatenation in loops is O(N²) — always use join()

modules.title: Testing, Design Patterns & SOLID

sections.heading: pytest — Professional Testing
sections.content: No code goes to production without tests. pytest is the industry standard for Python testing. Companies like Google and Amazon test everything.
sections.tip: pytest.raises() is how you test that errors are raised correctly. Every production function that can fail should have a test for its error case.

sections.heading: Mocking — Test Without Real APIs
sections.content: Mocking replaces real objects with fake ones during testing. Use it when your code calls external APIs, databases, or services you don't want to hit during tests.
sections.code:
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
import requests

# Function that calls external API
def get_user(user_id):
    response = requests.get(f'https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}')
    if response.status_code == 200:
        return response.json()
    return None

# Test WITHOUT hitting real API
@patch('requests.get')
def test_get_user(mock_get):
    # Setup fake response
    mock_response = MagicMock()
    mock_response.status_code = 200
    mock_response.json.return_value = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
    mock_get.return_value = mock_response

    # Test
    result = get_user(1)
    assert result['name'] == 'Alice'
    mock_get.assert_called_once_with('https://api.example.com/users/1')

@patch('requests.get')
def test_get_user_not_found(mock_get):
    mock_response = MagicMock()
    mock_response.status_code = 404
    mock_get.return_value = mock_response

    result = get_user(999)
    assert result is None
sections.tip: @patch replaces the IMPORT path, not the function location. Common mistake: patching 'requests.get' when you should patch 'mymodule.requests.get'.

sections.heading: Design Patterns — Singleton & Factory
sections.content: Design patterns are reusable solutions to common software problems. Interviewers test these to see if you can architect large systems.
sections.tip: Don't overuse patterns. Use Singleton for shared resources (DB, config). Use Factory when you have multiple classes implementing the same interface.

sections.heading: SOLID Principles in Python
sections.content: SOLID principles make code maintainable when 50+ developers work on the same codebase. These are essential for senior/lead engineer interviews.
sections.tip: typing.Protocol is Python's answer to interfaces. It enables duck typing WITH type safety — the best of both worlds.

key_points: pytest is the industry standard — use assert, parametrize, and fixtures
@patch mocks external dependencies — never hit real APIs in tests
Singleton ensures one instance — use for DB connections and configs
Factory pattern creates objects without specifying exact class
SOLID makes code maintainable — Dependency Injection is the most important principle

modules.title: Docker, CI/CD & Production Python

sections.heading: Virtual Environments — Why They Matter
sections.content: Every Python project should have its own virtual environment. Without it, package conflicts between projects will break things. This is the FIRST thing any interviewer checks in your project.
sections.code:
# Create virtual environment
python -m venv myproject_env

# Activate it
# Windows:
myproject_env\Scripts\activate
# Mac/Linux:
source myproject_env/bin/activate

# Install packages (isolated to this env)
pip install flask requests pandas

# Save dependencies
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Anyone can recreate your environment:
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Deactivate
deactivate

# requirements.txt example:
# flask==3.0.0
# requests==2.31.0
# pandas==2.1.0
sections.tip: Never install packages globally with pip install on your system Python. Always use a virtual environment. This is non-negotiable in professional development.

sections.heading: Type Hints — Write Self-Documenting Code
sections.content: Type hints make your code readable, enable IDE autocomplete, and catch bugs before runtime. Modern Python (3.9+) supports native type hints without importing typing for basic types.
sections.tip: Type hints are NOT enforced at runtime — they're for documentation and static analysis. Use mypy to catch type errors before running code.

sections.heading: Docker — Package Your App for Deployment
sections.content: Docker ensures your app runs the same everywhere — your laptop, your colleague's machine, and production servers. Every senior developer must know Docker basics.
sections.code:
# Dockerfile — the recipe for your container

# Stage 1: Build
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Production (smaller image)
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
COPY . .

# Don't run as root in production
RUN useradd --create-home appuser
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["python", "app.py"]

# Build and run:
# docker build -t myapp .
# docker run -p 8000:8000 myapp

# .dockerignore file (like .gitignore):
# __pycache__
# *.pyc
# .env
# venv/
# .git
sections.tip: Multi-stage builds keep images small. Stage 1 installs dependencies, Stage 2 copies only what's needed. This can reduce image size from 1GB to 100MB.

sections.heading: CI/CD — Automated Testing with GitHub Actions
sections.content: CI/CD automatically runs tests, lints code, and deploys when you push to GitHub. No professional team ships code without CI/CD.
sections.code:
# File: .github/workflows/python-ci.yml

name: Python CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: '3.11'

    - name: Install dependencies
      run: |
        python -m pip install --upgrade pip
        pip install -r requirements.txt
        pip install pytest flake8 mypy

    - name: Lint with flake8
      run: flake8 . --max-line-length=120

    - name: Type check with mypy
      run: mypy . --ignore-missing-imports

    - name: Run tests
      run: pytest -v --tb=short

# This runs automatically on every push!
# Green checkmark = all tests pass
# Red X = something broke — fix before merging
sections.tip: Set up CI/CD on Day 1 of any project. It catches bugs before they reach production. Even solo developers should use it.

sections.heading: Profiling & Debugging — Find Bottlenecks
sections.content: Before optimizing, MEASURE. Premature optimization is the root of all evil. Use profiling tools to find what's actually slow.
sections.code:
# timeit — measure small code snippets
import timeit

# Which is faster: list comprehension or map?
t1 = timeit.timeit('[x**2 for x in range(1000)]', number=10000)
t2 = timeit.timeit('list(map(lambda x: x**2, range(1000)))', number=10000)
print(f'Comprehension: {t1:.4f}s')
print(f'Map: {t2:.4f}s')
# Comprehension is usually faster!

# cProfile — profile entire program
import cProfile

def slow_function():
    total = 0
    for i in range(1000000):
        total += i ** 2
    return total

cProfile.run('slow_function()')
# Shows: number of calls, time per call, cumulative time

# Memory profiling
# pip install memory_profiler
from memory_profiler import profile

@profile
def memory_heavy():
    big_list = [i for i in range(1000000)]
    big_dict = {i: i**2 for i in range(1000000)}
    return len(big_list) + len(big_dict)

# pdb — interactive debugger
import pdb

def buggy_function(data):
    pdb.set_trace()  # Execution pauses here
    # Or use: breakpoint()  # Python 3.7+
    result = process(data)
    return result
# Commands: n(next), s(step into), c(continue), p(print), q(quit)
sections.tip: Use timeit for micro-benchmarks, cProfile for finding bottlenecks in large programs, and memory_profiler when your app uses too much RAM.

key_points: Always use virtual environments — never install packages globally
Type hints catch bugs before runtime — use mypy for static analysis
Multi-stage Docker builds reduce image size by 90%
CI/CD runs tests automatically on every push — catches bugs early
Profile before optimizing — timeit for snippets, cProfile for programs

quiz.question: What is the output of print(type([]))?
quiz.explanation: [] creates an empty list, so type([]) returns <class 'list'>

quiz.question: What does 'hello'[::-1] return?
quiz.explanation: [::-1] reverses the string using slice with step -1

quiz.question: Which of these is immutable?
quiz.explanation: Tuples cannot be modified after creation. Lists, dicts, and sets are mutable.

quiz.question: x = [1,2,3]; y = x; y.append(4); print(len(x))
quiz.explanation: y = x makes y reference the same list. Modifying y also modifies x.

quiz.question: dict.get('key', 'default') when key missing?
quiz.explanation: .get() returns the default value if key not found, unlike dict['key'] which raises KeyError.

quiz.question: What is bool('')?
quiz.explanation: Empty string is falsy. Empty list, dict, set, 0, None are also falsy.

quiz.question: Which adds element to END of list?
quiz.explanation: append() adds single element at end. extend() adds multiple. insert() adds at index. add() is for sets.

quiz.question: Correct way to create empty set?
quiz.explanation: {} creates empty dict, not set. set() is correct for empty set.

quiz.question: print(2 ** 3 ** 2) output?
quiz.explanation: ** is right-associative. 2 ** (3 ** 2) = 2 ** 9 = 512

quiz.question: What does enumerate() return?
quiz.explanation: enumerate() returns iterator of (index, element) tuples.

quiz.question: def func(a, b=[]): b.append(a); return b — func(1) then func(2)?
quiz.explanation: Mutable default arguments are shared across calls. Same list b is reused.

quiz.question: @staticmethod vs @classmethod?
quiz.explanation: @classmethod receives cls (the class). @staticmethod receives no implicit first parameter.

quiz.question: x = [1,2,3,4,5]; print(x[1:4:2])?
quiz.explanation: x[1:4:2] — start 1, end before 4, step 2. Indices 1 and 3 = values 2 and 4.

quiz.question: Correct way to copy list without reference?
quiz.explanation: old[:], old.copy(), and list(old) create shallow copies. new = old just creates another reference.

quiz.question: What does *args represent?
quiz.explanation: *args collects extra positional arguments into a tuple. **kwargs collects keyword args into dict.

quiz.question: [i for i in range(5) if i % 2 == 0] output?
quiz.explanation: Filters even numbers from range(5). 0, 2, 4 are even.

quiz.question: What is a generator?
quiz.explanation: Generators use yield to produce values one at a time, saving memory.

quiz.question: a={1,2,3}; b={2,3,4}; print(a & b)?
quiz.explanation: & is intersection — elements common to both sets.

quiz.question: What happens with 'with open()' if exception occurs?
quiz.explanation: Context manager's __exit__ is called even if exception occurs, closing the file.

quiz.question: nonlocal x in inner function does what?
quiz.explanation: nonlocal allows inner function to modify variable in enclosing scope.

quiz.question: D(B,C) with super().__init__() — MRO order?
quiz.explanation: Python MRO (C3 linearization): D → B → C → A. Each __init__ called once.

quiz.question: [lambda i=i: i for i in range(5)] — call each?
quiz.explanation: Default arg i=i captures current value at each iteration. Without it, all would be 4.

quiz.question: What does __slots__ do?
quiz.explanation: __slots__ replaces per-instance __dict__ with fixed attributes, using 40-50% less memory.

quiz.question: print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)?
quiz.explanation: Floating point precision: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004. Use math.isclose() instead.

quiz.question: What is a metaclass?
quiz.explanation: Metaclass defines how classes are created. type is the default metaclass.

quiz.question: Counter('abracadabra').most_common(2)?
quiz.explanation: 'a' appears 5 times, 'b' 2 times. Why 'b' over 'r' (both 2)? Counter preserves insertion order since Python 3.7+ — 'b' appears before 'r'.

quiz.question: GIL workaround for CPU-bound tasks?
quiz.explanation: GIL allows only one thread for Python bytecode. Use multiprocessing for CPU tasks. Threading works for I/O because GIL releases during I/O.

quiz.question: g=gen(); next(g); next(g) — yields 1,2,3?
quiz.explanation: Each next() resumes generator from where it left off. First yields 1, second yields 2.

quiz.question: Python garbage collection uses?
quiz.explanation: Reference counting for most objects. Cyclic GC handles circular references that reference counting misses.

quiz.question: with MyClass() as obj — __exit__ sets value=0. print(obj.value) after with?
quiz.explanation: Inside with: value is 42. After with: __exit__ sets to 0. Variable obj still exists outside with block.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of 'x in my_list'?
quiz.explanation: List membership check scans every element — O(N). Use set for O(1) lookup.

quiz.question: deque.popleft() vs list.pop(0) — which is faster?
quiz.explanation: deque.popleft() is O(1). list.pop(0) is O(N) because it shifts all remaining elements.

quiz.question: itertools.groupby requires input to be?
quiz.explanation: groupby only groups CONSECUTIVE equal elements. Input must be sorted by the grouping key first.

quiz.question: What is typing.Protocol used for?
quiz.explanation: Protocol enables structural subtyping — if a class has the required methods, it satisfies the Protocol without explicit inheritance.

quiz.question: What does @patch do in testing?
quiz.explanation: @patch temporarily replaces a real object with a mock during tests, so you don't hit real APIs or databases.

quiz.question: Which is an anti-pattern in Python?
quiz.explanation: String concatenation with += in a loop is O(N²) because strings are immutable — each += creates a new string. Use ''.join() instead.

quiz.question: Multi-stage Docker build is used to?
quiz.explanation: Stage 1 installs dependencies and builds. Stage 2 copies only what's needed. This can reduce image from 1GB to 100MB.

quiz.question: asyncio.gather() does what?
options: Runs tasks concurrently and waits for all
quiz.explanation: gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently and returns when all complete. Total time = longest task, not sum of all.

quiz.question: What is Dependency Injection?
options: Passing dependencies as arguments instead of hardcoding
quiz.explanation: DI means a class receives its dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally. This makes code testable and flexible.

quiz.question: pip freeze > requirements.txt does what?
options: Saves all installed packages with versions to a file
quiz.explanation: pip freeze lists all installed packages with exact versions. Redirecting to requirements.txt lets others recreate your exact environment.
